questions.text: I believe that knowing what I want and going after it is a sign of maturity.
I prefer that group deliverables be finalized by whoever the group entrusts with that task.
I notice when a person offers to help not out of generosity but to position themselves as indispensable.
The suffering of strangers in distant places affects me in a way that feels personal and immediate.
I tend to notice my emotional reactions in retrospect rather than being gripped by them in the moment.
I find that I do not instinctively sense when I have overstayed my welcome.
I do not naturally sense when two people are having a power struggle disguised as polite conversation.
I think that approaching suffering with methodical clarity is the approach that most reliably converts concern into tangible progress.
I hold the view that self-sacrifice is one of the highest forms of contribution.
I believe my vivid emotional life is what makes me feel most alive.
My emotional equilibrium is rarely disrupted for long.
I work forward from where I am, letting deadlines exert their pull as they approach.
I feel more in control when I'm free to react to what's actually happening rather than following a script.
I have been able to maintain focus on a task even when someone nearby was visibly upset.
I consider it my strength that I can make hard calls quickly.
I feel more in control when I have a plan, even if it needs to be adjusted later.
I experience loss as something I can hold without it consuming me.
I get restless in meetings where no one is driving toward a conclusion.
I rarely notice when someone's contribution to a conversation is being systematically overlooked.
I hold the view that my own needs are the baseline I should secure before extending help outward.
I feel that when I take care of my own needs, I am better equipped for everything else.
I believe that emotional intensity is one of my defining characteristics.
I prefer a supportive role in negotiations and high-stakes conversations.
I am highly aware of the social dynamics playing out in digital communication, not just in-person.
I separate my work into distinct phases with clear transitions between them.
I am the kind of person who can be brought to tears by a commercial.
I believe that living for something beyond myself is the only way to find real meaning.
I figure things out each time rather than writing down how I did it before.
I think that when I analyze distressing circumstances with rational precision, I honor the seriousness of the situation more effectively than if I were emotionally swept up in it.
I feel responsible for ensuring that meetings have concrete outcomes.
I like to establish ground rules before starting a collaborative effort.
I usually wait for someone else to propose a plan before weighing in.
I believe that the moral pull of suffering should translate directly into changed behavior, not just into emotional acknowledgment.
I find that the stories I most love telling are about times I was able to help someone.
I hold the view that the moral claim of suffering is the most fundamental ethical reality — everything else is commentary.
I find that I intuitively map social networks, understanding who influences whom.
